---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 16: Corner solution outcomes and censored regression models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 16](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-16-corner-solution-outcomes-and-censored-regression-models/).

Textbook: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*, MIT Press.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

# Data

```{julia}
mroz = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("wooldridge/mroz"))
size(mroz)
```

## Example 16.3 (mroz.dta)

Annual hours worked is a corner-solution outcome: 325 of the 753 married women
report zero hours. The example (p. 527) contrasts OLS with the Tobit MLE,
constructs a Tobit R-squared from the conditional-mean prediction
`E[y | x] = Φ(xβ/σ)·xβ + σ·φ(xβ/σ)`, and finally compares two nested Tobit
specifications with `fitstat`.

```{julia}
#=
use mroz, clear

reg hours nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6

tobit hours nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6, ll(0)

* get the R-squared for tobit model
matrix b = e(b)
local se = el(b,1, 9)
di `se'

predict xb, xb
gen yhat = norm(xb/`se')*xb + `se'*normden(xb/`se')
reg hours yhat

* comparing two tobit models
tobit hours nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6, ll(0)
fitstat, saving(m0)

tobit hours nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6  unem city, ll(0)

fitstat, using(m0)
=#
```

### OLS on hours

```{julia}
XH = [:nwifeinc, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6]
hours_ols = stata_regress(mroz; y = :hours, x = XH)
```

**Comparison.** Every coefficient and standard error reproduces the published
output exactly (`nwifeinc` −3.446636 / 2.544 … `_cons` 1330.482 / 270.7846),
together with `Number of obs = 753`, `F(7,745) = 38.50`, `R-squared = 0.2656`
and `Root MSE = 750.18`.

### Tobit, `ll(0)`

```{julia}
tb = stata_tobit(mroz, :hours, XH; ll = 0.0, vce = :oim)
```

```{julia}
Printf.@printf("_se (sigma)      = %.7g   (Std. Err. %.7g)\n", tb.sigma, tb.se_σ)
Printf.@printf("Log likelihood   = %.4f\n", tb.ll)
Printf.@printf("left-censored    = %d\nuncensored       = %d\n",
               sum(Float64.(mroz.hours) .<= 0), sum(Float64.(mroz.hours) .> 0))
```

The header statistics Stata prints (`LR chi2(7)` and pseudo R²) need the
intercept-only Tobit, which we fit here as well:

```{julia}
tb_null = stata_tobit(mroz, :hours, Symbol[]; ll = 0.0, quiet = true)
LR7 = 2 * (tb.ll - tb_null.ll)
Printf.@printf("Log-Lik Intercept Only = %.4f\n", tb_null.ll)
Printf.@printf("LR chi2(7)             = %.2f\n", LR7)
Printf.@printf("Prob > chi2            = %.4f\n",
               1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(7), LR7))
Printf.@printf("Pseudo R2              = %.4f\n", 1 - tb.ll / tb_null.ll)
```

**Comparison.** Coefficients match Stata to seven significant digits
(`nwifeinc` −8.814243, `educ` 80.64561, `exper` 131.5643, `expersq` −1.864158,
`age` −54.40501, `kidslt6` −894.0217, `kidsge6` −16.218, `_cons` 965.3053),
σ̂ = 1122.022 with SE 41.57905 (Stata 41.57903), log-likelihood −3819.0946,
`LR chi2(7) = 271.59`, pseudo R² 0.0343, and the 325 / 428 censoring split.
The standard errors differ only in the sixth significant digit (e.g. `nwifeinc`
4.459053 vs Stata's 4.459096) because `stata_tobit` forms the observed
information from a finite-difference Hessian rather than Stata's analytic one.

### Tobit R-squared

```{julia}
Φ(z) = Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), z)
φ(z) = Distributions.pdf(Distributions.Normal(), z)

xb  = tb.X * tb.β
σ̂   = tb.sigma
Printf.@printf("di `se' = %.4f\n", σ̂)

tobit_fit = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    hours = Float64.(mroz.hours),
    yhat  = Φ.(xb ./ σ̂) .* xb .+ σ̂ .* φ.(xb ./ σ̂))

r2_reg = stata_regress(tobit_fit; y = :hours, x = [:yhat])
```

**Comparison.** `di \`se'` gives 1122.0217 as in Stata. The auxiliary
regression reproduces `R-squared = 0.2742`, `F(1,751) = 283.78`,
`Root MSE = 742.78`, `yhat` = .9634450 (Stata .9634449) with SE .0571918, and
`_cons` = 45.52784 with SE 49.34596. So the Tobit conditional mean explains
27.4% of the variation in hours, against the OLS 26.6%.

### Comparing the two Tobit models

```{julia}
tb2 = stata_tobit(mroz, :hours, vcat(XH, [:unem, :city]); ll = 0.0, vce = :oim)
Printf.@printf("\n_se (sigma) = %.7g    Log likelihood = %.4f\n", tb2.sigma, tb2.ll)
```

```{julia}
lrt = stata_lrtest(tb2.ll, tb.ll, 2)
```

`fitstat` (Long & Freese) is provided by StatEcon (src/econ_helpers.jl):
`stata_fitstat` computes the fit statistics for a fitted tobit against the
intercept-only log-likelihood, and `fitstat_table` prints the
Current / Saved / Difference comparison (Stata's `fitstat, saving(m0)` then
`fitstat, dif(m0)`). For the tobit the latent error variance is σ̂², the default.

```{julia}
N   = tb.n
fs0 = stata_fitstat(tb,  tb_null.ll, N)   # saved model (m0)
fs1 = stata_fitstat(tb2, tb_null.ll, N)   # current model
fitstat_table(fs1, fs0; prob_lr_dif = lrt.p)
```

**Comparison.** The larger Tobit reproduces Stata's coefficients to six or seven
significant digits (`unem` −23.34072 vs −23.3407, `city` 11.36005 vs 11.36026,
`_cons` 1060.113 vs 1060.127), σ̂ = 1119.474 and log-likelihood −3817.8867 —
all matching. Standard errors again differ only in the last printed digits,
except `city`, where the FD-Hessian OIM gives 100.6341 against Stata's 99.61766
(≈1% apart); `city` is the flattest direction of the likelihood
(t ≈ 0.11), so it is the coefficient most sensitive to the numerical Hessian.

The likelihood-ratio comparison matches the published `fitstat` block:
log-likelihood difference 1.208, `LR difference = 2.416` on 2 d.f.,
`Prob > LR: 0.299`, and a BIC difference of **10.833** in favour of the saved
(smaller) model. `unem` and `city` do not belong in the equation. The
reconstructed `fitstat` panel reproduces every published line, including
`Variance of y*` (1956786.689 here vs 1956786.696 in Stata — a last-digit
consequence of σ̂ agreeing only to ~9 digits), `Variance of error`
(1258932.622 vs 1258932.624),
`McKelvey and Zavoina's R2 = 0.357/0.359`, `AIC = 10.168/10.170`,
`BIC = 2709.885/2720.717` and `BIC' = -225.226`.
